covered_sections: Classification of Physical Quantities>Scalar and Vector Quantities

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which instrument is used to measure the volume of a liquid in a laboratory?
questions.hint: Volume of liquid is measured with a container that has volume markings.
questions.explanation: A graduated cylinder is designed to measure liquid volume accurately. Option B measures force, C measures length, D measures time.

explanations_tiered.definition: Volume is the amount of space occupied by a substance, often measured in liters or milliliters.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Graduated cylinders have calibrated markings to read liquid volume by observing the meniscus.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A graduated cylinder is like a marked cup that tells you exactly how much liquid is inside.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Liquid volume is measured by pouring into a graduated cylinder and reading the scale at eye level to avoid parallax error.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Volume measurement uses displacement or direct reading; graduated cylinders provide precision typically ±1% when used correctly with meniscus reading at bottom of curve.
reasoning_steps: Identify what is being measured: volume of liquid.
Recall instruments for volume: graduated cylinder, beaker, pipette.
Choose the most appropriate for general lab use.
misconceptions: Using a beaker for precise volume — beakers are less accurate.
Confusing volume measurement with mass or length instruments.

glossary_entry.definition: Instrument for measuring liquid volume with marked increments.

flashcard.front: What measures liquid volume in a lab?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Name an instrument for liquid volume.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show actual graduated cylinder and meniscus reading.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Graduated cylinder measures liquid volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the seven fundamental physical quantities?
questions.hint: Fundamental quantities are independent; force depends on mass, length, and time.
questions.explanation: Force is a derived quantity (\(F = ma\)), not fundamental. Temperature, amount of substance (mole), and luminous intensity (candela) are fundamental.

explanations_tiered.definition: Fundamental physical quantities are independent base quantities from which all others are derived.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The seven fundamentals are: length, mass, time, temperature, current, amount of substance, luminous intensity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Force is not a base quantity because it comes from mass times acceleration.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Force is derived: \(F = ma\). It depends on mass (fundamental), length, and time (through acceleration).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The SI defines seven base quantities; force has dimension \(MLT^{-2}\), showing dependence on fundamentals.
reasoning_steps: Check each option against the list.
Identify the one not in the list.
misconceptions: Thinking force is fundamental because it's common — it is derived.
Confusing amount of substance (mole) with mass.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: 7 fundamentals: length, mass, time, temp, current, mole, candela.

glossary_entry.definition: Independent base quantity in SI system.

flashcard.front: List the seven fundamental physical quantities.
flashcard.back: Length, mass, time, temperature, current, amount of substance, luminous intensity.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No, force is derived from mass × acceleration.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Classify physical quantities as fundamental or derived
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Force is derived from mass, length, and time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Pressure is defined as force per unit area: \(P = \frac{F}{A}\). What is the SI unit of pressure?
questions.hint: Force is in newtons, area in square meters.
questions.explanation: Pressure = force (N) / area (m²) = N/m², also called pascal (Pa). Option A is joule (energy); B is surface tension; D is not correct for pressure.

explanations_tiered.definition: Pressure is the force applied perpendicular to a surface per unit area.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The SI unit of pressure is the pascal (Pa), which equals one newton per square meter.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Pressure measures how much force is spread over an area. One newton per square meter is one pascal.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(P = F/A\), so unit = N/m², given the special name pascal (Pa).
reasoning_steps: Substitute units: N for force, m² for area.
misconceptions: Confusing pressure with force — force is N, pressure is N/m².
Using N·m (joule) which is energy, not pressure.

glossary_entry.definition: SI unit of pressure, equal to one newton per square meter.

flashcard.front: What is the SI unit of pressure?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the unit of pressure?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine units of derived physical quantities

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: How many centimeters are there in 2.5 kilometers?
questions.explanation: 2.5 km = 2.5 × 1000 m = 2500 m. 2500 m × 100 cm/m = 250,000 cm. Option C omits one factor of 10.

explanations_tiered.definition: Length conversion uses multiplication by powers of ten based on prefixes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Convert km to m (×1000), then m to cm (×100), so total ×100,000.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Kilometers to centimeters: two steps — first to meters, then to centimeters.
reasoning_steps: Convert km to m: multiply by 1000.
Convert m to cm: multiply by 100.
misconceptions: Stopping after km to m (2500 cm) — forgot second step.
Multiplying by 1000 then dividing by 100.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bag of rice has a mass of 2.5 kg. What is this mass in grams?
questions.explanation: 2.5 kg × 1000 g/kg = 2500 g. Option B misses a zero; A misses two zeros; D multiplies by 10,000.

explanations_tiered.definition: Kilo means 1000, so kilogram is 1000 grams.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: To convert kilograms to grams, multiply by 1000.
explanations_tiered.beginner: One kilogram is 1000 grams, so 2.5 kilograms is 2500 grams.
misconceptions: Dividing by 1000 instead of multiplying.
Using 1 kg = 100 g (incorrect).

glossary_entry.definition: Unit of mass equal to 0.001 kg.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: How many seconds are there in 2.5 hours?
questions.explanation: 2.5 h × 3600 s/h = 9000 s. Option A is 0.5 hours; C is 2.5 × 6000 (incorrect factor); D is 2.5 × 1000.

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Multiply hours by 3600 to get seconds.
explanations_tiered.beginner: One hour has 60 minutes, each minute has 60 seconds, so 1 hour = 3600 seconds.
misconceptions: Using 1 h = 60 s (forgetting minutes).
Multiplying by 60 only once.

glossary_entry.definition: Unit of time equal to 3600 seconds.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to half-day or movie length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Force is defined as mass times acceleration: \(F = m \times a\). The unit of force, the newton (N), is equivalent to which combination of base units?
questions.explanation: Mass (kg) × acceleration (m/s²) = kg·m/s² = 1 N. Option A is momentum; B is joule; D is not a standard unit.

explanations_tiered.definition: Newton is the SI unit of force, defined as the force needed to accelerate 1 kg at 1 m/s².
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: From \(F = ma\), unit = kg × m/s² = kg·m/s².
explanations_tiered.beginner: Force = mass × acceleration; so unit is kilogram times meter per second squared.
reasoning_steps: Substitute base units: kg for m, m/s² for a.
misconceptions: Confusing newton with joule (kg·m²/s²).
Using kg·m/s (momentum unit).

flashcard.front: What is 1 newton in base units?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following is a fundamental physical quantity?
questions.hint: Fundamental quantities are independent base quantities.
questions.explanation: Electric current (ampere) is one of the seven fundamental quantities. Velocity, density, and pressure are derived from fundamentals.

explanations_tiered.definition: Electric current is the flow of electric charge; its SI unit is the ampere.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Current is fundamental because it is defined independently and not derived from other quantities.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Ampere is a base unit, like meter for length or kilogram for mass.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Fundamental quantities include length, mass, time, temperature, current, amount of substance, and luminous intensity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The ampere is defined via the force between two parallel conductors, not derived from other base units.
reasoning_steps: Recall list of seven fundamental quantities.
Check each option against the list.
misconceptions: Thinking velocity is fundamental — it is derived (length/time).
Confusing density as fundamental — it is mass/volume.

glossary_entry.definition: Flow of charge; fundamental quantity, unit ampere.

flashcard.front: Is electric current fundamental or derived?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Name a fundamental quantity besides length, mass, time.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss other fundamentals: mole, candela, kelvin.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Classify physical quantities as fundamental or derived
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Electric current (ampere) is fundamental.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Work is defined as force times displacement: \(W = F \times d\). What is the SI unit of work?
questions.hint: Force in newtons, displacement in meters.
questions.explanation: Work = force × displacement, so unit = N·m, also called joule (J). Option A is N/m (surface tension); B is impulse; D is pressure.

explanations_tiered.definition: Work is energy transferred by force acting over displacement; unit is joule.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Work = force × displacement, so unit = N × m = N·m = joule (J).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Joule is the unit of energy and work; it equals newton times meter.
reasoning_steps: Substitute units: N for F, m for d.
misconceptions: Confusing work with torque (also N·m but different context).
Using N·s (impulse) or N/m (surface tension).

glossary_entry.definition: SI unit of work and energy, equal to N·m.

flashcard.front: What is the SI unit of work?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Joule is equivalent to which product?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to lifting weight against gravity.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine units of derived physical quantities

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A room is 5 meters wide. Express this width in millimeters.
questions.explanation: 5 m × 1000 mm/m = 5000 mm. Option C is 5 × 100; B is 5 × 10; A no conversion.

explanations_tiered.definition: Milli means one-thousandth, so 1 m = 1000 mm.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Multiply meters by 1000 to get millimeters.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Each meter has 1000 millimeters, so 5 meters have 5000 millimeters.
misconceptions: Using 1 m = 100 mm (confusing with cm).
Dividing by 1000 instead of multiplying.
